merger_name: Australian Venue Co - leasehold and assets of Brabham Tavern, WA
merger_description:
AVC Operations Pty Ltd (**AVC**) proposes to acquire all of the assets of the licensed tavern intending to trade as Brabham Tavern, from Koy Pty Ltd acting as trustee for ABC Property Trust (**Vendor**) under an Asset Sale Agreement which includes the benefit of a lease of Brabham Tavern to AVC, and AVC will also acquire the liquor licence over the venue from Brabtav Pty Ltd, a connected entity of the Vendor (the **Acquisition**).

AVC is a subsidiary of Australian Venue Co. Holdings Limited which operates over 200 licensed venues throughout Australia and New Zealand, primarily pubs, as well as some bars, clubs and licensed restaurants. AVC's venues are individually branded and are mostly located in inner-city and metro areas, offering a range of products and services including food, alcoholic and non-alcoholic beverages, function rooms, live entertainment and gaming facilities.

Brabham Tavern will be a newly built turnkey pub / contemporary licensed venue located at 45 Palfrey St, Brabham WA 6055. It is to be a typical Australian tavern with a public bar, bistro, outdoor courtyard and a private event space. It is still under development and has not yet begun trading.

events.title: Notification waiver determination published
events.determination_commission_division: Determination made by Commissioner Williams pursuant to a delegation under section 25(1) of the Act

determination_table_content.details:
AVC Operations Pty Ltd (AVC), a subsidiary of Australian Venue Co.
Holdings Limited, applied for a notification waiver in respect of its
proposed acquisition of the leasehold and assets of the new
licensed tavern intending to trade as Brabham Tavern, as described
in the transaction documents provided as part of the application (the
Acquisition).

The Australian Competition and Consumer Commission has
determined under section 51ABV(1)(a) of the Competition and
Consumer Act 2010 (Cth) that the Acquisition is not required to be
notified.

The acquirer, AVC, operates over 200 licensed venues throughout
Australia and New Zealand, primarily pubs, as well as some bars,
clubs and licensed restaurants. AVC's venues are individually
branded and are mostly located in inner-city and metro areas,
offering a range of products and services including food, alcoholic
and non-alcoholic beverages, function rooms, live entertainment and
gaming facilities.
The target, Brabham Tavern is a newly built turnkey pub /
contemporary licensed venue located at 45 Palfrey St, Brabham, WA.
It is still under development and has not yet begun trading.
The parties both supply dining and on-premises liquor in the Perth
metropolitan area in Western Australia.

In making this notification waiver determination, the Australian
Competition and Consumer Commission (the ACCC) has considered
the information provided with the notification waiver application and
had regard to the factors in section 51ABV(2)(b) of the Competition
and Consumer Act 2010 (Cth) (the Act).
Based on the information provided in the application, the ACCC
considers that the Acquisition is unlikely to give rise to any material
lessening of competition. In particular:
a. there is no horizontal overlap between AVC and the Brabham
Tavern in the supply of dining and on-premises liquor in the
local area surrounding the Brabham Tavern
b. there are alternative providers of dining and liquor for on-site
consumption in the local area surrounding the Brabham
Tavern and in the broader Perth metropolitan area.
The ACCC has also had regard to the likelihood that, if the
Acquisition were put into effect, the notification thresholds
determined under section 51ABP(1) of the Act would apply.
While the ACCC considers that the notification thresholds are likely
to be met, given that material competition concerns are unlikely to
arise, the ACCC has determined that the Acquisition is not required
to be notified.
The ACCC considers that the determination is consistent with the
object of the Act and the interests of consumers in promoting
competition.
For more information about the ACCC’s approach to considering
notification waiver applications and to assessing competition
effects more generally, see the ACCC’s interim guidance on
notification waivers and merger assessment guidelines.
